draft.introduction: Chapter 22 develops the logic of reversal that runs throughout the Tao Te Ching: the bent becomes straight, the empty becomes full, the worn becomes new. The sage embraces the One and becomes a pattern for the world.

translation.text: If you want to become whole, let yourself be partial. If you want to become straight, let yourself be crooked. If you want to become full, let yourself be empty. If you want to be reborn, let yourself die. If you want to be given everything, give everything up. The sage embraces the One and becomes the pattern of the world.
translation.original_text: 曲则全 枉则直 洼则盈 敗则新

translator_notes.note: The logic of reversal here closely parallels the Christian paradox of kenosis (self-emptying) and the Buddhist teaching of sunyata (emptiness). Whether these represent independent discoveries of a universal spiritual principle or historical cross-pollination is one of the great questions of comparative religion.
